turns.name: JOHN G. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: Justice Gorsuch has the opinion of the Court in Case 24-362 Martin v. United States.

turns.name: NEIL M. GORSUCH
turns.text: In the pre-dawn hours of October 18, 2017, the FBI raided the wrong house in suburban Atlanta. Officers meant to execute a search and arrest warrant at a suspected gang hideout on Landau Lane. Instead, they stormed a quiet family home on Denville Trace occupied by Hilliard Cliatt, his partner Curtrina Martin and her 7-year-old son. A SWAT team breached the front door and detonated a flashbang grenade. Fearing a home invasion, Mr. Cliatt and Ms. Martin hid in a bedroom closet,but the officers soon found them, threw them on the ground and trained their weapons on them. Only then did they realize they were in the wrong house. The cause of the mistake? One of the officers blames his personal GPS device. No one can confirm that account, though, because the officer threw his device away not long after the raid. It seems too that the officers neither checked the street sign nor the house number before they launched their raid. Left with personal injuries and property damage, but no compensation, Mr. Cliatt and Ms. Martin sued the United States for damages, asserting claims for various negligent and intentional torts. Under background legal principles, the United States enjoys sovereign immunity. That immunity generally precludes suits like this one. But some time ago, Congress adopted the Federal Tort Claims Actor FTCA, for short. That law does two things. First, it waives the government's sovereign immunity and allows suits like this one to proceed. But as it turns out, that waiver itself is subject to 13 exceptions that effectively restore the government's immunity in certain situations. Second, for cases that are not barred by one of those 13 exceptions, the FTCA sets forth a rule of liability. That rule provides that the government may be held accountable on the same terms as a private individual under the law of the place where the misconduct occurred. Most courts apply those directives as written, but the Eleventh Circuit has adopted an outlier approach to both the FTCA's immunity and its liability rules, an approach that eventually led it to dismiss Mr. Cliatt's and Ms. Martin's suit. We took this case to address both of those outlying rules, and we hold that neither is appropriate. I will not bore you with the details of why we reject the Eleventh Circuit's approach to the FTCA's immunity rules. But the reason why we reject the 11th Circuit's approach to the Act's liability rules might be a little more interesting. Remember, that rule provides that the government may be held to account on the same terms as a private individual under the law of the place where the alleged misconduct occurred. Now, under that rule, you might think that this suit could proceed. Surely, after all, any private litigant who launched a nighttime raid on a neighbor's house would be liable under the Law of Georgia or likely anywhere else in the country. But the Eleventh Circuit saw it differently. It believed that a precedent of this courtcalled In re Neagle compelled it to dismiss this suit. To appreciate why that assessmentwas mistaken, a little background about that extraordinary case might help. In re Neagle involved an affair, a homicide, and a habeas petition. In 1883, Sarah Hill claimed to be the wife of a United Statessenator named William Sharon and sought a share of his fortune in acrimonious California divorce proceedings. Sharon admitted to an affair but insisted that Hill had forged the pair's handwritten marriage contract. Hill hired David Terry to represent her. Terry was a former Chief Justice of the California Supreme Court, but had resigned that post after he had killed another U.S. Senator in a duel. As the litigation wore on, Hill and Terry, lawyer and client, married. Eventually, the dispute between Hill and Sharon wound up before U.S. Supreme Court Justice Stephen Field while he was riding circuit in California. Now, Terry, the lawyer, and Field were no strangers. They had in fact served together on the California Supreme Court. Even so, Justice Field issued a devastating ruling against Hill. And as he announced his decision, Hill leapt from her seat, denounced the justice's bought, and had to be carried from the courtroom. Joining the fracas, her husband Terry punched a marshal and brandished a bowie knife. Even after the couple spent time in jail for contempt, they continued to issue threats against Justice Field. All those events found their way into the U.S. Reports this way. Aware of the threat that Hill and Terry posed, the U.S. Attorney General ordered Deputy Marshal David Neagle, himself a former Chief of Police in Tombstone, Arizona, and a contemporary of Wyatt Earp, to accompany Justice Field the next time he rode circuit in California. That decision proved prescient, for Terry soon quartered Justice Field on a train and attacked him, intervening to protect the Justice, Neagle shot and killed Terry. After the shooting, California authorities arrested Neagle and began prosecuting him for murder. Neagle countered by filing a petition for a writ ofhabeas corpus in federal courts seeking his release. When that habeas petition reached this court, the justices agreed the writ should issue. They reasoned that the Constitution's Supremacy clause shielded Neagle from state criminal charges. Without some such protection, the court concluded, California could effectively frustrate federal law by prosecuting a U.S. marshal for an act authorized by the laws of the United States and one he had to perform to carry out his duties. Now, as memorable as In re Neagle may be, we do not see how it informs the prosaic task of applying the FTCA as the Eleventh Circuit thought. The case may stand for the proposition that federal law will sometimes preempt a state criminal law when it conflicts with an officer's duties. But In re Neagle does not speak to a situation where, as here, Congress has expressly boundthe federal government to accept liability under state law on the same terms as a private individual. After all, no private individual could deploy In re Neagle to his advantage. It has only ever worked to shield federal officers who are discharging their duties. The upshot? The Eleventh Circuit was wrong to dismiss this case based on In re Neagle. Accordingly, we vacate its decision and remand the matter for further proceedings. The decision of the court is unanimous. Justice Sotomayor has filed a concurring opinion.
